intro.kicker: Topic 16 of 22 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: A uniform chain of length l is placed on a rough table with length l’ hanging over the edge. Friction on the portion on the table must balance the weight of the hanging portion. The standard result uses the fact that for a uniform chain, mass is proportional to length, giving the coefficient of friction μ = l’ / (l − l’). Rearranging, the maximum fraction of chain that can hang without sliding is l’/l = μ/(1+μ). NEET tests this as a numerical or a conceptual question about what fraction can overhang given a specific μ.

expectedQuestions.description: Single-formula numerical in NEET: find the maximum length l’ of chain that can hang given l and μ, or find μ given the fraction overhung. Also tested as: what fraction of a uniform chain can hang off a table if μ = 0.25? (Answer: 1/5 = 0.2).

timeRequired.description: Quick single-concept topic. 10 min to understand why mass ∝ length for uniform chain, 10 min to derive μ = l’/(l − l’), 10 min on 3–4 NEET-pattern substitutions. Commit the fraction form l’/l = μ/(1+μ) to memory for instant recall.

difficulty.description: The derivation is direct but students often forget to simplify to l’/l form. The typical NEET trap is finding l’ given l and μ and making arithmetic errors. Remember that l = l’ + l_table, so l’ = μ l / (1+μ). For μ = 1/3: l’ = l/4. For μ = 1/2: l’ = l/3.

nriUsaGap.description: US AP Physics does not test hanging-chain friction problems. This is a standard application of friction equilibrium to a continuous object (chain). The conceptual step that matters is: for a uniform chain, the fraction of mass hanging equals the fraction of length hanging. Once that is understood, the friction condition mg_hanging = μmg_table reduces to the length ratio formula.

weightage.sectionTitle: Maximum Length of Hung Chain — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Appears in alternate NEET years; typically 1 question per appearance.
Most directly tests the fraction l’/l = μ/(1+μ) with a numerical μ.
Occasionally asked as: find the minimum μ for a given l’/l ratio.

strategySteps.title: Understand Why Length = Mass Fraction
strategySteps.description: A uniform chain has constant linear mass density λ = m/l (kg/m). The mass of any segment of length x is λx. So mass ratio = length ratio. This is why μ = mass_hanging / mass_table directly becomes μ = l’ / (l − l’).

strategySteps.title: Derive Both Forms of the Result
strategySteps.description: μ = l’/(l − l’) (direct form). Rearranged: l’ = μ(l − l’) = μl − μl’. So l’(1+μ) = μl, giving l’/l = μ/(1+μ). Commit both forms to memory.

strategySteps.title: Spot the NEET Numerical Shortcut
strategySteps.description: Quick reference: μ = 1/4: l’ = l/5. μ = 1/3: l’ = l/4. μ = 1/2: l’ = l/3. μ = 1: l’ = l/2. For any μ, l’ = μl/(1+μ). Always check: should l’ < l? Yes, since μ/(1+μ) < 1 for all finite μ.

cards.title: Hung Chain Formula Sheet
cards.description: Derivation of μ = l’/(l − l’) with diagram, rearranged forms, table of l’/l for common μ values (1/4, 1/3, 1/2, 3/4, 1), and 4 solved NEET-pattern numericals.

cards.title: Chain Problems Practice Set
cards.description: 8 MCQs covering both forms of the formula, non-uniform chain variants (for conceptual testing), and multi-step problems involving both chain and pulley.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Maximum Length of Hung Chain

cards.title: Hung Chain Equilibrium
cards.coreRevision: For a uniform chain of length l, let l’ overhang the table and (l − l’) rest on it. At limiting equilibrium: weight of hanging part = maximum static friction. Since λ is constant: λg l’ = μ λg(l − l’). Cancelling λg: μ = l’/(l − l’). Rearranging: l’ = μl/(1+μ).
points: Valid only for uniform chain (constant linear density)
If chain is non-uniform, mass fractions ≠ length fractions

curriculum.hintText: How AP Physics compares to NEET on hung-chain friction problems.

gaps.title: Continuous Object Friction Not in AP Scope
gaps.description: AP Physics does not systematically apply friction analysis to extended continuous objects like chains. The conceptual step that mass-fraction = length-fraction for a uniform chain is not tested. NEET expects students to both know this equivalence and apply it to find l’.
bullets: Memorise: for uniform chain, mass ∝ length, so μ = l’/(l − l’).
Derive l’ = μl/(1+μ) for exam-ready substitution.

questions.question: A uniform chain of length 4 m is kept on a rough table (μ = 0.25). The maximum length of chain that can hang over the edge without sliding is:
A hanging length of l/4 of a uniform chain on a rough table is in equilibrium. The coefficient of friction is:
A uniform chain of length 10 m lies on a table (μ = 0.5). Maximum overhang length is:
What fraction of a uniform chain can hang from a table when μ = 2/3?
For a uniform chain, if l’ = l/3, the coefficient of friction is:
The formula μ = l’/(l − l’) is valid only when:

items.title: Write both forms of the formula
items.description: μ = l’/(l − l’) and l’ = μl/(1+μ). Know which to use: if μ is given and l’ is sought, use the second form. If l’ is given and μ is sought, use the first.

items.title: Check the uniformity assumption
items.description: The key physical premise is constant linear density λ. If the chain were non-uniform, the mass ratio would not equal the length ratio. NEET problems that specify uniform chains can use the length formula directly.

items.title: Quick-check numerical values
items.description: μ = 1/4: l’ = l/5. μ = 1/3: l’ = l/4. μ = 1/2: l’ = l/3. μ = 1: l’ = l/2. Memorise these for instant MCQ verification.

items.question: Why does the formula use length ratios directly instead of mass?
items.answer: For a uniform chain, mass = λ × length where λ is the constant linear mass density. Both the hanging mass (λ l’) and the table mass (λ(l − l’)) have the same λ, which cancels when you write the friction equilibrium condition. So the mass ratio = length ratio, allowing the formula to be stated entirely in terms of lengths.

items.question: What happens if l’ > μl/(1+μ)?
items.answer: If the hanging length exceeds the maximum l’ = μl/(1+μ), the friction force cannot support the weight of the hanging portion. The chain slides and continues to slide (kinetic friction now applies) until it falls off the table.

items.question: Can this approach be used for non-uniform chains?
items.answer: Not directly. For a non-uniform chain, linear mass density λ(x) varies with position x. The mass of the hanging and on-table portions must be calculated by integrating λ(x) over the respective segments. The simple ratio μ = l’/(l − l’) does not apply.

grounding_summary: Page 243 presents the Maximum Length of Hung Chain problem. A uniform chain of length l has l’ hanging over the table edge. Using the result that for a uniform chain, mass ratio = length ratio, the equilibrium condition gives μ = l’/(l − l’). This is derived from the hanging-mass friction result: μ = m_2/m_1, directly substituting lengths for masses because λ is constant.

The page explicitly uses the note: ‘As chain have uniform linear density’ to justify the length-ratio substitution, making the derivation a single-step application of the earlier surface-pulley friction result.
grounding_excerpt: Since the chain have uniform linear density therefore the ratio of mass and ratio of length for any part of the chain will be equal.
The length of path in both the cases are same.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Class 11 Part 1 — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion (Friction)
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source establishing friction equilibrium principles used in the chain-on-table derivation.

sources.title: NEET Physics Friction MCQs Set A — StudiesToday
sources.why_used: Provides NEET-level MCQ context for the hung-chain problem including standard numerical substitutions tested in previous NEET papers.

review.notes: Content grounded on page 243 OCR. Formula mu = l'/(l-l') derived from uniform chain property. Both forms l'/l = mu/(1+mu) included. Limiting cases verified. Scope within chapter 05 friction.
